chunk_summary: Guidance on addressing common nutrigenomics claims in pediatric care, emphasizing evidence-based responses to avoid unnecessary dietary restrictions and promote balanced nutrition.
learning_objectives: Understand the lack of clinical utility for routine MTHFR testing and its implications for treatment.
Learn evidence-based responses to common nutrigenomics claims, such as 'detox genes' or 'gluten genes.'
Develop strategies to promote balanced nutrition and avoid unnecessary dietary restrictions in pediatric patients.
key_facts: Routine MTHFR testing has minimal clinical utility and does not justify high-dose folate or restrictive diets (ACMG guideline).
HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positivity indicates susceptibility to celiac disease, not diagnosis; a negative result makes celiac disease unlikely.
Vitamin D supplementation should follow guideline doses based on age, risk factors, and measured deficiency; avoid chronic megadoses.
Common 'obesity genes' or 'carb sensitivity' alleles have small effects and do not replace standard lifestyle interventions.
There are no validated pediatric genotypes requiring detox diets, chelation, or routine supplements.
reference_quote: "Routine MTHFR testing has minimal clinical utility and does not justify high-dose folate, anticoagulation, or restrictive diets." (ACMG guideline: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23288205/)
document_key_topics: Guidelines for addressing dietary concerns in children
